description: This figure presents Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) characterization results of low-density δ-ferrite steel samples rolled at four different temperatures: R650 (650℃), R750 (750℃), R850 (850℃), R950 (950℃). It contains four sets of subgraphs: (a-d) Grain Boundary (GB) combined with Grain Orientation Spread (GOS) maps, where gray lines represent 2-15° low-angle grain boundaries, black lines represent >15° high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs), and green regions indicate areas with GOS ≤2°; (e-h) Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps, reflecting local misorientation and dislocation density; (i-l) Inverse Pole Figure (IPF) maps, showing grain orientation distribution; (m-p) IPF maps overlaid with nanoindentation marks, used to measure nanohardness of different phases. Key trends observed: low-temperature rolled samples (R650, R750) show higher KAM values (0.48-0.55°) corresponding to higher dislocation density, HAGB proportions of 43.5-49.6%, and GOS ≤2° proportions of 6.9-12.3%; high-temperature rolled samples (R850, R950) have lower KAM values (0.3-0.4°), increased HAGB proportions of 60.1-75.7%, higher GOS ≤2° proportions of 19.4-25.8%, and grain orientation shifted from <001> to <101> dominant. Nanoindentation results show the hardness difference between phases is only 0.72 GPa for low-temperature rolled samples, while it reaches 3.86 GPa for high-temperature rolled samples, which easily causes stress concentration at phase boundaries. This figure supports the conclusion that rolling temperature regulates the microstructure and mechanical properties of the tested steel: low-temperature rolling with dispersed ultra-fine spheroidized carbides improves strength and toughness via Orowan dislocation pinning, while large hardness mismatch in high-temperature rolled samples degrades impact toughness.
